events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is of Polish-American descent, a retired steel mill worker from Pittsburgh, PA. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history (quit 12 years ago), moderate alcohol use (2-3 drinks/week), BMI 27.4, ECOG performance status 1 at diagnosis. Past medical history includes hypertension (controlled on lisinopril 10mg daily), hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin 40mg daily), and type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled, HbA1c 6.8%). No family history of colorectal cancer. He is married with two adult children.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3 N2a M1a) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C18.7, histology code 8140/3: Adenocarcinoma, NOS). Summary stage: Distant (metastatic). Primary tumor location: sigmoid colon, 18 cm from anal verge by colonoscopy. Synchronous liver metastases: 4 lesions in bilateral hepatic lobes (largest 3.2 cm in right lobe segment VI), consistent with M1a disease (liver-only at initial staging). Relevant biomarkers: KRAS exon 2 codon 12 G12D mutation (pathogenic); NRAS wild-type; BRAF wild-type (non-V600E); HER2 not amplified; MMR proficient / microsatellite stable (pMMR/MSS) by IHC (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+); CEA at diagnosis: 148.2 ng/mL; CA 19-9: 312 U/mL. Confirmation: Positive histology (colonoscopic biopsy of sigmoid mass). Tumor size (primary): 4.1 cm. Primary tumor was not resected at time of diagnosis (synchronous presentation with unresectable liver mets). Tumor sidedness: Left-sided. Tumor differentiation: Moderately differentiated.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from colonoscopic biopsy of sigmoid colon mass. Gross description: Multiple biopsy fragments, 0.3-0.8 cm in aggregate. Microscopic findings: Invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with tubular and focal cribriform architecture. Tumor cells with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasm. Intraluminal necrotic debris present (dirty necrosis). No signet ring cell features. Lymphovascular invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy. Perineural invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy. IHC panel: CDX2 positive (diffuse, strong), CK20 positive, CK7 negative, consistent with colorectal primary. MMR proteins: MLH1 retained, MSH2 retained, MSH6 retained, PMS2 retained (pMMR/MSS). Diagnosis: Invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV and oral contrast (staging CT CAP) performed. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Circumferential sigmoid colon wall thickening measuring 4.1 x 3.8 cm with mucosal enhancement and pericolonic fat stranding. Several pericolonic lymph nodes enlarged up to 1.5 cm (N2a disease). No evidence of local invasion beyond colonic wall. LIVER: Four hepatic lesions identified: (1) Segment VI right lobe, 3.2 x 2.8 cm, hypoenhancing with peripheral rim enhancement; (2) Segment IVb, 1.8 x 1.6 cm; (3) Segment II left lobe, 1.4 x 1.1 cm; (4) Segment VII, 0.9 x 0.8 cm. Imaging characteristics consistent with metastatic deposits. LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules identified. LYMPH NODES: No distant lymphadenopathy. PERITONEUM: No peritoneal carcinomatosis. BONES: No osseous metastases. Impression: Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. Potentially resectable liver disease contingent on chemotherapy response. No extrahepatic metastases identified. Baseline imaging for treatment response assessment.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for colorectal adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the sigmoid colon (primary tumor biopsy). Platform: Foundation One CDx (comprehensive genomic profiling). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 5 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12D mutation (allele frequency 42%, pathogenic, known oncogenic driver; RAS pathway activator; excludes benefit from EGFR-targeted therapies); TP53 p.R248W mutation (allele frequency 48%, pathogenic, loss-of-function); APC p.E1554* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 51%, pathogenic, truncating, loss of tumor suppressor function). Co-mutations and additional findings: SMAD4 p.R361H mutation (allele frequency 38%, likely pathogenic, TGF-beta pathway alteration; may be associated with worse prognosis in stage IV CRC); PIK3CA exon 20 H1047R mutation not detected; BRAF V600E not detected; NRAS exon 3 codon 61 wild-type; ERBB2 (HER2) copy number: not amplified (CN 2.1); MET copy number: not amplified; CDKN2A homozygous deletion detected; FBXW7 p.R505C mutation (allele frequency 22%, likely pathogenic); Copy number alterations: chromosome 8q gain (MYC locus), chromosome 18q loss (SMAD2/SMAD4 locus), chromosome 20q gain. Gene fusions: None detected. Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 14% (intermediate). POLE/POLD1: wild-type. RET: wild-type. NTRK1/2/3 fusions: not detected. Summary: KRAS G12D mutant, BRAF wild-type, pMMR/MSS colorectal adenocarcinoma. Not a candidate for anti-EGFR therapy. No targetable alterations identified beyond standard chemotherapy regimens.

At age 58 years, the patient had an initial oncology consultation and multidisciplinary tumor board review. Diagnosis: Stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. Tumor board discussion: Liver disease is technically resectable but borderline given bilobar involvement. Decision to pursue conversion chemotherapy with FOLFOX plus bevacizumab with intent to reassess hepatic resectability after 4-6 cycles. Primary tumor is asymptomatic; no immediate need for diverting surgery. CEA 148.2, CA 19-9 312. Patient counseled on goals of therapy (potentially curative intent if conversion to resectable disease achieved), treatment side effects (peripheral neuropathy with oxaliplatin, hypertension with bevacizumab, myelosuppression), and the importance of monitoring hepatic function. Patient is agreeable and motivated. ECOG PS 1. Plan: Initiate FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab. Port-a-cath placement arranged. Baseline labs reviewed: CBC WNL, CMP WNL, LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 52, AST 48, likely from liver metastases). Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Baseline blood pressure 128/78 mmHg on lisinopril.

At age 58 years, the patient underwent port-a-cath placement (subcutaneous implantable venous access device, right chest wall). Procedure performed by interventional radiology. No intraoperative complications. Port accessed and flushed without difficulty. Chest X-ray post-procedure confirmed appropriate port position, no pneumothorax.

At age 58 years, the patient initiated palliative/conversion-intent systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab (Cycle 1 Day 1). Regimen: Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV over 30-90 min; Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours (concurrent); 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus; 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days. Premedications: Ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, palonosetron for delayed nausea prophylaxis. Cycle 1 administered without acute infusion reactions. Patient discharged home with 5-FU pump and antiemetic prescriptions.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into treatment, after Cycle 3 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin-induced). Symptoms: Mild tingling and numbness in fingertips, bilateral, worse with cold exposure (cold-induced dysesthesias characteristic of acute oxaliplatin neurotoxicity). Also noted: Grade 1 fatigue, Grade 1 nausea (well-controlled with ondansetron). No dose reduction required at this time. Counseled on cold avoidance and use of gloves. Blood pressure noted at this visit: 148/92 mmHg -- new Grade 1 hypertension likely related to bevacizumab. Lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily. Blood pressure target: <140/90 mmHg.

At age 58 years (after 4 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 2 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast for interim response assessment. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass mildly decreased in size, now measuring 3.6 x 3.2 cm (previously 4.1 x 3.8 cm). Pericolonic lymph nodes decreased in number and size (largest now 0.9 cm). LIVER: All four hepatic lesions demonstrate interval decrease in size: (1) Segment VI lesion now 2.6 x 2.2 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm); (2) Segment IVb now 1.3 x 1.2 cm (previously 1.8 x 1.6 cm); (3) Segment II now 1.0 x 0.9 cm (previously 1.4 x 1.1 cm); (4) Segment VII now 0.7 x 0.6 cm (previously 0.9 x 0.8 cm). LUNGS: No new pulmonary nodules. No new metastatic disease. Response assessment by RECIST 1.1: Partial response (PR). Sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 28% from baseline. CEA: 67.4 ng/mL (down from 148.2 at baseline). Impression: Partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Hepatic disease remains bilobar. Multidisciplinary reassessment recommended after completion of planned 6 additional cycles.

At age 58 years (after 4 cycles, interim imaging review), the patient had an oncology office visit for treatment assessment. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Imaging confirms partial response with 28% reduction in hepatic and primary tumor burden. CEA trending down. Patient tolerating treatment reasonably well with Grade 1 neuropathy (fingertip tingling, cold dysesthesias), Grade 1 hypertension now better controlled on increased lisinopril dose (BP today 136/84), Grade 1 fatigue. Performance status remains ECOG 1. Plan: Continue FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for planned total of 12 cycles. Repeat tumor board review after Cycle 8 to reassess hepatic resectability. Neuropathy monitoring ongoing -- will reduce oxaliplatin dose by 25% or discontinue if neuropathy progresses to Grade 2 or higher. Patient and spouse in good spirits, motivated to continue.

At age 58 years (approximately 5 months into treatment, after Cycle 7 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (cumulative oxaliplatin-induced neurotoxicity). Symptoms: Persistent numbness and tingling in fingers and toes bilaterally, now present at rest (no longer only cold-triggered), mild difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts). Neurologic examination: Decreased vibratory sense in bilateral great toes. Action taken: Oxaliplatin dose reduced by 25% (from 85 mg/m2 to 64 mg/m2) per ASCO guidelines. Patient counseled on cumulative neuropathy risk. Bevacizumab and 5-FU doses maintained. Blood pressure: 134/82 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg -- well-controlled. Grade 1 alopecia noted (partial). Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, mild, does not require dose reduction. Neurology referral placed for neuropathy evaluation and management.

At age 58 years (after 8 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 4 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass now 3.1 x 2.7 cm (previously 3.6 x 3.2 cm post-Cycle 4), mild further reduction. Pericolonic nodes no longer enlarged. LIVER: Further reduction in all four lesions: (1) Segment VI: 2.0 x 1.7 cm; (2) Segment IVb: 0.9 x 0.8 cm; (3) Segment II: 0.7 x 0.6 cm; (4) Segment VII: 0.5 x 0.4 cm. LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules identified. No new distant metastases. Response assessment (RECIST 1.1): Continued partial response, cumulative 42% decrease in sum of target lesion diameters from baseline. CEA: 38.1 ng/mL (continued downtrend). Impression: Ongoing partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Liver disease showing continued treatment response. Tumor board review recommended: hepatic metastasectomy may be feasible for the dominant Segment VI lesion; however, Segment IVb, II, and VII lesions remain present and multifocal resection carries high risk. Consider continued chemotherapy vs. ablative approaches.

At age 58 years (multidisciplinary tumor board re-review after 8 cycles), the patient was discussed at the hepatobiliary and colorectal oncology multidisciplinary tumor board. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Discussion: Continued hepatic response but four separate liver lesions remain across bilateral lobes. Hepatic Surgery assessment: Resection technically feasible with R0 potential for Segment VI lesion; however, Segments II and IVb involvement would require extensive hepatectomy with marginal future liver remnant (FLR estimated 28%). FOLFOX-associated hepatotoxicity (sinusoidal obstruction) also a concern given 8-cycle exposure. Decision: Continue chemotherapy for 4 additional cycles (total 12 planned) before further resectability reassessment. Consider portal vein embolization if FLR remains marginal. If maximal response achieved, reassess for staged hepatectomy vs. radiofrequency ablation for smaller lesions. Neuropathy: Grade 2, oxaliplatin dose-reduced 25%. Will proceed with final 4 cycles at reduced dose. Patient agrees with plan.

At age 58 years (after completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 6 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for end-of-induction response assessment. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass now 2.8 x 2.4 cm with decreased mural thickening and improved mucosal appearance. Pericolonic nodes resolved. LIVER: (1) Segment VI: 1.6 x 1.4 cm (significantly reduced); (2) Segment IVb: 0.7 x 0.5 cm (near-radiographic CR); (3) Segment II: 0.5 x 0.4 cm (near-radiographic CR); (4) Segment VII: Not definitively visualized (possible radiographic CR). Overall RECIST response: Confirmed partial response, 52% reduction in target lesion sum from baseline. CEA: 22.8 ng/mL (continued downtrend from 148.2 baseline). CA 19-9: 89 U/mL (down from 312 baseline). LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules. No new distant metastatic disease. Impression: Best response to induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab: confirmed partial response. Sigmoid primary tumor reduced but residual disease present. Hepatic disease significantly reduced with three of four lesions near or at radiographic complete response. Segment VI dominant lesion remains identifiable.

At age 58 years (completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, end-of-induction assessment), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment transition planning. Cancer present, with partial response to induction therapy. Per plan, oxaliplatin discontinued after 12 cycles due to cumulative neurotoxicity and reaching planned treatment endpoint. Neuropathy currently Grade 2, persistent at rest, bilateral hands and feet -- improvement expected over time following oxaliplatin discontinuation. Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg, acceptable control. Performance status: ECOG 1. Tumor board reviewed imaging: residual sigmoid primary tumor (likely still harboring viable tumor given partial response only), Segment VI liver lesion 1.6 cm, three smaller lesions near-CR. Decision: Transition to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (per OPTIMOX strategy -- capecitabine considered but patient preference for infusional 5-FU given adequate port access). Will continue bevacizumab to maintain disease control. Re-evaluate for surgical consolidation at 3 months of maintenance if neuropathy improves. Patient counseled on maintenance strategy and prognosis.

At age 58 years (6 months after initial diagnosis), the patient transitioned to maintenance systemic therapy with 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (simplified LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab). Regimen: Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days (biweekly). Oxaliplatin omitted due to cumulative Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. Rationale: Maintenance strategy per OPTIMOX1 trial concept -- oxaliplatin holiday, continuation of 5-FU/bevacizumab backbone to consolidate response and delay progression. Goal: Maintain partial response achieved with induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab, preserve quality of life, allow neuropathy recovery.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into maintenance therapy, 8 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a follow-up neurology appointment for oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy. Assessment: Neuropathy has improved from Grade 2 to Grade 1 following oxaliplatin discontinuation. Patient reports residual mild tingling in fingertips and toes but improved fine motor function (can button shirt again without difficulty). Neurologic exam: Reduced but improved vibratory sense in toes. Nerve conduction studies: Mild sensory neuropathy pattern consistent with oxaliplatin-induced injury. Recommendation: Continue monitoring, physical therapy for balance exercises, no additional pharmacologic intervention needed at this time. Prognosis for further gradual improvement over 6-12 months. Blood pressure on maintenance: 132/80 mmHg, well-controlled. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome mild/stable. Patient tolerating maintenance well with Grade 1 fatigue.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 months from initial diagnosis, after approximately 4 months of maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for routine response surveillance. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass appears stable compared to prior CT, measuring 2.9 x 2.5 cm (minimally changed from 2.8 x 2.4 cm). No bowel obstruction. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 2.1 x 1.8 cm -- INCREASED from 1.6 x 1.4 cm (31% increase, unequivocal progression). Segment IVb: 1.0 x 0.9 cm -- new apparent growth from 0.7 x 0.5 cm. Segment II: 0.6 x 0.5 cm -- minimally changed. LUNGS: NEW FINDINGS: Three new bilateral pulmonary nodules identified: (1) Right lower lobe, 0.8 x 0.7 cm; (2) Left upper lobe, 0.6 x 0.5 cm; (3) Right middle lobe, 0.5 x 0.4 cm. Morphology and clinical context consistent with pulmonary metastases. IMPRESSION: Disease progression by RECIST 1.1 -- new pulmonary metastases (new lesion = PD regardless of primary/hepatic lesion changes) and hepatic progression. This represents a significant change from prior partial response. CEA: 67.3 ng/mL (up from 22.8 at end of induction). CA 19-9: 187 U/mL (up from 89). Clinical urgency: Prompt oncology reassessment and second-line therapy planning indicated.

At age 58 years (10 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an urgent oncology visit following surveillance imaging demonstrating disease progression. Cancer present, progressing. CEA risen to 67.3 (previously 22.8). New pulmonary metastases identified (three bilateral nodules) plus hepatic progression of Segment VI and IVb lesions. This represents progression on maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab, consistent with oxaliplatin-free interval reintroduction pattern. Assessment: Second-line disease progression; patient is now stage IV M1b (liver and lung metastases). Performance status: ECOG 1 (maintained). Labs reviewed: CBC -- mild anemia (Hgb 10.8), ANC 2.1, platelets 198; CMP -- LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 68, AST 62, likely from hepatic progression). Creatinine 0.9. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.18 (borderline, monitoring for proteinuria given continued bevacizumab exposure). Discussion: Second-line therapy options reviewed including FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab (ECOG E3200 precedent) vs. FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (RAISE trial, demonstrated OS benefit with ramucirumab vs. placebo in mCRC post-FOLFOX). Given biologic durability question with continued bevacizumab, decision made to switch to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Patient counseled regarding prognosis and realistic goals of care in second-line setting. Patient understands goals are palliative, wishes to continue aggressive treatment.

At age 58 years (10 months from initial diagnosis), the patient initiated second-line systemic chemotherapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cycle 1 Day 1). Regimen: Ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV over 60 min (given first); Irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours (concurrent with irinotecan); 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus; 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days. Premedications: Ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, atropine 0.25 mg IV (for irinotecan-related cholinergic syndrome prophylaxis). Loperamide prescription provided for delayed diarrhea management. Rationale: RAISE trial demonstrated OS benefit of ramucirumab plus FOLFIRI vs. FOLFIRI alone in second-line mCRC (median OS 13.3 vs. 11.7 months, HR 0.844, p=0.022). Prior anti-VEGF therapy with bevacizumab; class switch to anti-VEGFR2 antibody (ramucirumab). Baseline blood pressure: 142/90 mmHg (mild elevation likely residual bevacizumab effect plus inherent hypertension risk).

At age 58 years (approximately 1 month into second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab therapy, 11 months from diagnosis), the patient developed Grade 2 hypertension exacerbation attributed to ramucirumab (VEGFR2 inhibition). Blood pressure readings in clinic: 158/96 mmHg, 162/98 mmHg. Home monitoring confirmed persistent elevation. This represents new-onset Grade 2 hypertension despite lisinopril 20 mg daily. Also noted: Grade 2 diarrhea (irinotecan-related), requiring loperamide 4 mg initially then 2 mg after each loose stool, up to 16 mg/day -- adequately controlled on this regimen. Grade 1 alopecia (expected with irinotecan). Grade 1 fatigue. UGT1A1 genotype: heterozygous *28/*1 (intermediate metabolizer), consistent with moderate irinotecan sensitivity -- no dose reduction required at current dose. Management of hypertension: Amlodipine 5 mg daily added to lisinopril 20 mg. Blood pressure target <140/90 mmHg. Recheck in 2 weeks. Ramucirumab dose held for one cycle pending blood pressure control.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into second-line therapy, 12 months from diagnosis), the patient returned for blood pressure reassessment and Cycle 3 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Blood pressure: 136/84 mmHg on dual antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg). Hypertension now Grade 1 -- adequately controlled. Ramucirumab dose resuming at full dose per protocol (held one cycle). Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.22 (acceptable, <2.0 threshold for ramucirumab hold). Diarrhea: Grade 1, managed with occasional loperamide. Neuropathy: Continued Grade 1 residual oxaliplatin neuropathy (mild tingling fingertips), not worsening with FOLFIRI (irinotecan does not cause neuropathy). Fatigue: Grade 1, manageable. Performance status: ECOG 1. CBC: Hgb 10.2 (mild anemia, Grade 1), ANC 1.8 (Grade 1 neutropenia, no febrile neutropenia), platelets 178. No dose reductions required at this time.

At age 58 years (approximately 4 months into second-line therapy, 14 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for response assessment on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass: 2.7 x 2.3 cm (minimally changed, stable). No bowel obstruction, no pericolonic complications. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 1.8 x 1.5 cm (decreased from 2.1 x 1.8 cm, improving); Segment IVb: 0.8 x 0.7 cm (decreased from 1.0 x 0.9 cm, improving); Segment II: 0.5 x 0.4 cm (stable/near-CR). LUNGS: Right lower lobe nodule: 0.7 x 0.6 cm (minimally decreased from 0.8 x 0.7 cm); Left upper lobe nodule: 0.5 x 0.5 cm (stable); Right middle lobe nodule: 0.4 x 0.3 cm (stable to slightly decreased). No new pulmonary nodules. No new metastatic sites identified. Response assessment by RECIST 1.1: Partial response -- 18% decrease in sum of target lesion diameters from nadir at progression. Overall assessment: Favorable response to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with hepatic and pulmonary disease stabilization and partial response. CEA: 44.1 ng/mL (decreased from 67.3 at progression). CA 19-9: 134 U/mL (decreased from 187).

At age 58 years (14 months from initial diagnosis, 4 months into second-line therapy), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment response review. Cancer present, responding to second-line therapy. Imaging demonstrates partial response to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with hepatic and pulmonary disease responding. CEA declining (44 from nadir 67). Patient tolerating treatment with manageable toxicity profile: Grade 1 diarrhea (loperamide as needed), Grade 1 hypertension (controlled on dual antihypertensive therapy), Grade 1 fatigue, residual Grade 1 oxaliplatin neuropathy (stable, not worsening). Performance status: ECOG 1. Quality of life: Patient reports continued ability to perform light household activities, occasional golf (modified), maintaining social functioning. He is retired and spends time with grandchildren. Psychosocial: Patient and wife remain engaged. Discussed clinical trial options -- no immediately available trials matching his profile at this institution. Plan: Continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Reassess imaging in 8 weeks. Ongoing antihypertensive management. Palliative care referral offered; patient defers at this time but open to future referral.

At age 59 years (approximately 7 months into second-line therapy, 17 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for continued response surveillance. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass: 2.8 x 2.5 cm (minimally increased, borderline stable vs. progression). Mild pericolonic haziness noted. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 1.9 x 1.7 cm (minimally increased from 1.8 x 1.5 cm, borderline stable); Segment IVb: 0.9 x 0.8 cm (minimally increased); Segment II: 0.6 x 0.5 cm (minimally increased). LUNGS: Right lower lobe nodule: 0.8 x 0.7 cm (stable); Left upper lobe nodule: 0.6 x 0.5 cm (minimal increase); Right middle lobe nodule: 0.4 x 0.4 cm (stable). No new pulmonary nodules or new sites of metastatic disease. Response assessment (RECIST 1.1): Stable disease (SD) -- no unequivocal progression, no new lesions, no significant decrease. Sum of target lesions borderline increased by 8% from prior nadir (not meeting 20% threshold for PD). CEA: 51.8 ng/mL (slight increase from 44.1). CA 19-9: 162 U/mL. Impression: Best response was PR, currently stable disease. Continued treatment monitoring recommended; close interval imaging to confirm stability vs. early progression.

At age 59 years (17 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology visit for ongoing disease management. Cancer present, stable disease on second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Imaging shows stable disease with borderline trend towards minimal progression. CEA trending slightly upward (51.8 vs. 44.1). Performance status: ECOG 1. Cumulative irinotecan toxicity: Grade 1 diarrhea (managed), Grade 1 fatigue. Hypertension: BP 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg, acceptable. Neuropathy: Grade 1 residual (oxaliplatin), stable, no worsening. Hematologic: Hgb 10.0, ANC 1.6, plt 165 -- mild myelosuppression, no febrile neutropenia events. Discussed disease trajectory with patient. Patient continues to have good quality of life. Reviewed third-line options in anticipation of future progression: regorafenib (CORRECT trial) or TAS-102/trifluridine-tipiracil (RECOURSE trial) are standard options in chemorefractory mCRC. No NTRK fusion, no HER2 amplification, no BRAF V600E -- no targeted therapy options available. Plan: Continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with close monitoring. Repeat imaging in 6 weeks given borderline findings.

At age 59 years (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a pathology result from CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule (0.8 cm), performed to confirm metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma and obtain tissue for repeat molecular profiling (ctDNA and tissue re-biopsy for potential acquired resistance mechanisms). Gross: Three core needle biopsy fragments, 0.8-1.2 cm. Microscopic: Sections show moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with morphology consistent with metastatic colorectal primary. Tumor cells arranged in glandular structures with focal dirty necrosis. IHC: CK20 positive (strong diffuse), CDX2 positive (moderate diffuse), CK7 negative, TTF-1 negative. Findings consistent with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma to lung (not a primary lung adenocarcinoma). Note: Specimen submitted for repeat next-generation sequencing to evaluate for acquired resistance mutations. Diagnosis: Metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary. Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule biopsy.

At age 59 years (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on a repeat biopsy specimen obtained from the right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis (post-treatment specimen, obtained after FOLFOX and FOLFIRI exposure). Platform: Foundation One CDx. TMB: 6 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (unchanged from baseline). RETAINED alterations from baseline: KRAS p.G12D (allele frequency 47%, retained oncogenic driver); TP53 p.R248W (allele frequency 51%, retained); APC p.E1554* (allele frequency 48%, retained); SMAD4 p.R361H (allele frequency 41%, retained); CDKN2A homozygous deletion (retained). NEW/ACQUIRED alterations compared to baseline primary tumor biopsy: KRAS p.G12V secondary subclonal mutation (allele frequency 11%, likely acquired, potential mechanism of resistance or tumor heterogeneity); PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (allele frequency 18%, likely acquired or newly detectable, PI3K pathway activation); MYC amplification (copy number gain, CN 8.2, increased from CN ~3 at baseline); RB1 p.Q494* truncating mutation (allele frequency 14%, likely acquired, loss of tumor suppressor). ERBB2: not amplified. VEGFR2/KDR: no amplification or activating mutation (ramucirumab target unaltered). FUSIONS: None detected. Summary: Evolution of molecular landscape with acquisition of potential resistance mechanisms (PIK3CA E545K, RB1 loss, MYC amplification, subclonal KRAS G12V). MSS phenotype retained. No actionable targetable alterations emerge beyond standard therapy. Clinical relevance: Molecular evolution consistent with clonal selection under chemotherapy pressure.

At age 59 years (18-19 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a comprehensive oncology visit integrating repeat molecular profiling results with clinical status. Cancer present, with stable disease on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab though borderline trend toward progression. Repeat NGS from pulmonary metastasis shows molecular evolution: PIK3CA E545K acquisition, MYC amplification, RB1 loss -- consistent with clonal evolution under chemotherapy selection. KRAS G12D retained as dominant clone; new KRAS G12V subclone identified suggesting intratumoral heterogeneity. MSS status unchanged -- no immunotherapy opportunity. No HER2 amplification, no NTRK fusion, no actionable target identified. Performance status: ECOG 1. Patient remains functional, engaged, living at home. Updated goals of care discussion: Patient understands disease is incurable; primary goals are prolonging survival while maintaining quality of life and functional independence. Agreed to continue current regimen until clear radiographic progression given stable disease. Third-line options (regorafenib, TAS-102/tipiracil, or clinical trial) discussed for future planning. Patient expressed interest in clinical trial if eligible at time of next progression. Social work referral accepted at this visit. Palliative care referral accepted. Advanced care planning discussion initiated: patient has healthcare proxy (wife), completed advance directive.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT NOTE -- TREATMENT ASSESSMENT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
ATTENDING: Hematology/Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, treatment review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, BRAF wild-type) presenting today for interim response assessment and toxicity review following 4 cycles of mFOLFOX-6 plus Avastin.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION:
Present: Colorectal Oncology, Hepatobiliary Surgery, Interventional Radiology, Diagnostic Radiology.

Hepatic Surgery Assessment (Dr. [Hepatobiliary Surgery]):
Technical resectability was reviewed in detail. The Segment VI lesion (2.0 x 1.7 cm) is the dominant remaining hepatic lesion and is technically resectable with a planned posterior right hepatectomy approach. However, the residual bilobar nature of disease (Segments VI, IVb, II, and VII all involved -- though the latter is near-complete radiographic response) continues to pose a challenge for a curative-intent single-stage resection. To achieve R0 margins on all four sites would require an extended right hepatectomy with resection of the segment II/IVb lesions, likely necessitating a two-stage hepatectomy approach or combination of hepatectomy with radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of smaller contralateral lesions.

Future liver remnant (FLR) estimate based on current volumetric imaging: approximately 28% of total liver volume. This is at the lower threshold of acceptable FLR (generally >25-30% in a non-cirrhotic liver with chemotherapy exposure), but the team noted that cumulative FOLFOX hepatotoxicity -- specifically oxali-associated sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS) -- is a real risk at 8 cycles, even without overt imaging correlates. A pre-operative liver biopsy or intraoperative assessment of parenchymal quality would be warranted if resection proceeds.

Portal vein embolization (PVE) of the right portal vein was discussed as an option to augment FLR hypertrophy if required prior to right hepatectomy. However, given the continued favorable treatment response and the availability of 4 more planned cycles, the surgical team felt it premature to commit to PVE at this time.

For the Segment II lesion and the near-resolved Segment VII lesion, image-guided ablation (RFA or microwave ablation) could be considered as an adjunct to formal hepatic resection, potentially making a planned two-stage approach unnecessary depending on response at the end of 12 total cycles.

Conclusion from surgical perspective: Resection not recommended at this juncture given marginal FLR, bilobar involvement, and ongoing chemotherapy response. Recommend completing planned 12-cycle course and reassessing with formal volumetric imaging and surgical consultation at that time.

Oncology Assessment:
Patient has shown robust and continued treatment response over 8 cycles. CEA downtrend remains favorable. The case for continuing oxali-based therapy to maximize hepatic response is strong; however, Grade 2 neuropathy at dose-reduced Eloxatin is a real concern for cumulative toxicity with 4 additional cycles, even at 64 mg/m2. Will monitor closely. Avastin to continue, as no significant proteinuria or non-healing wound concerns.

- FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cyramza): Supported by the RAISE trial, which demonstrated an OS benefit for ramucirumab plus FOLFIRI versus FOLFIRI alone in patients who progressed on first-line bevacizumab-containing therapy (median OS 13.3 months vs. 11.7 months, HR 0.844, p=0.022). Ramucirumab targets VEGFR2 directly (extracellular domain antibody), providing a class switch from the anti-VEGF-A mechanism of bevacizumab.
- Regorafenib or TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf): These are third-line or later options; not appropriate at this juncture with good PS and multiple effective options remaining.
- Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab): CONTRAINDICATED given KRAS G12D mutation.
- Clinical trials: Patient is interested. I will review our current institutional portfolio for any relevant second-line CRC trials. No immediately available match identified today, but will reach out to trial coordinators.

Decision: After thorough discussion with the patient and his wife, we will proceed with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. I believe switching the anti-angiogenic class to ramucirumab makes biological and clinical sense given the RAISE data. Irinotecan is a reasonable second-line backbone given prior oxaliplatin exposure. UGT1A1 genotype will be checked prior to irinotecan initiation to guide dosing (heterozygous *28 would not necessarily prompt dose reduction at standard doses but needs to be known).

Prior to initiating: Labs repeated today reviewed above. Port is in place. We will plan Cycle 1 Day 1 for next week after confirming UGT1A1 results and reviewing with the patient.

2. HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure today 142/90 mmHg. On lisinopril 20 mg daily (previously increased for bevacizumab-related hypertension). Will add amlodipine 5 mg daily at this visit. Target BP <140/90 mmHg. Ramucirumab also carries hypertension risk, so close BP monitoring will be essential going forward. Instruct patient to check BP at home daily and contact the office if readings are consistently above 150/95. We will recheck at each treatment visit.

3. ANEMIA
Mild anemia (Hgb 10.8). Likely multifactorial -- treatment-related, chronic disease, possible nutritional component. No signs or symptoms of significant anemia-related compromise at this time. Will monitor; if Hgb drops below 8.0 or patient becomes symptomatic, will consider erythropoiesis-stimulating agents or transfusion support.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (RESIDUAL OXALIPLATIN)
Currently Grade 1 by patient report, consistent with the neurology assessment showing gradual improvement since oxaliplatin discontinuation. Irinotecan does not cause peripheral neuropathy, so the remaining neuropathy should not worsen with FOLFIRI. Will continue monitoring. Physical therapy/balance program referral remains active.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT
Glucose mildly elevated today at 112 mg/dL. Diet-controlled T2DM. Dexamethasone will be used as premedication with FOLFIRI cycles -- counsel patient on transient steroid-related glucose elevations. Continue diet and lifestyle modifications. Check HbA1c at next visit.

6. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL
Patient was clearly distressed by today's news. We spent significant time discussing the implications of disease progression and the realistic goals of care in the second-line setting. He understands that his disease is not curable but that second-line treatment can provide meaningful disease control and survival benefit. He remains motivated and wants to continue aggressive treatment. His wife was present for the full discussion and is engaged and supportive. Social work referral previously deferred; patient is now amenable to this -- referral placed today. Palliative care referral also offered; patient agrees to a consultation. Advanced care planning discussion initiated -- will encourage him to complete or review advance directive at next visit.

FOLLOW-UP: Patient will return in approximately 1 week for Cycle 1 Day 1 FOLFIRI/ramucirumab after confirmatory UGT1A1 result review. Nurse coordinator will call with scheduling. Emergency contact and 24-hour oncology line reiterated.

Time spent with patient and family in discussion of results, prognosis, and treatment planning: approximately 45 minutes.

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "sigmoid colon biopsy," are multiple tan-pink to hemorrhagic tissue fragments ranging from 0.3 to 0.8 cm in greatest dimension (aggregate greatest dimension: approximately 1.6 cm). The fragments are firm and irregular. The specimen is entirely submitted in one cassette.

Cassette summary:
A1: All fragments, entirely submitted.

---

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Sections of A1 show multiple biopsy fragments of colonic mucosa extensively replaced and infiltrated by invasive adenocarcinoma. The tumor exhibits a predominantly tubular architecture with focal areas of cribriform gland formation. The neoplastic glands are irregularly shaped, back-to-back, with markedly reduced intervening stroma. Luminal spaces contain abundant necrotic cellular debris ("dirty necrosis"), a feature characteristic of colorectal-type adenocarcinoma. No signet ring cell morphology is identified. No mucinous differentiation exceeding 50% of tumor area is seen.

Tumor cells demonstrate moderate nuclear pleomorphism with enlarged, hyperchromatic, and irregularly contoured nuclei. Prominent single to multiple nucleoli are present. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is increased. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. The cytoplasm is moderate in amount and variably eosinophilic to amphophilic.

The background colonic mucosa shows reactive changes. No dysplastic polyp is identified in the submitted fragments.

Lymphovascular invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy material.
Perineural invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy material.
Deep invasive margin: Not assessable on endoscopic biopsy.

Nuclear grade: Intermediate (Grade 2 of 3, moderately differentiated).

---

ANCILLARY STUDIES:

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed in the Department of Immunohistochemistry, this institution):

A panel of immunohistochemical stains was performed on representative sections of tumor. Results are as follows:

  CDX2:        POSITIVE -- diffuse, strong nuclear staining in tumor cells
  CK20:        POSITIVE -- diffuse, strong cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells
  CK7:         NEGATIVE -- no significant staining in tumor cells
  Villin:      POSITIVE -- diffuse apical/cytoplasmic staining (confirmatory)

Interpretation: The immunoprofile (CDX2+/CK20+/CK7-) is consistent with a colorectal primary adenocarcinoma. A primary lung, upper GI, or pancreatic adenocarcinoma is not favored.

MISMATCH REPAIR (MMR) PROTEIN IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (reflexively performed per institutional colorectal adenocarcinoma protocol):

  MLH1:  RETAINED (positive nuclear staining in tumor cells; internal stromal and lymphocytic positive controls intact)
  MSH2:  RETAINED (positive nuclear staining in tumor cells)
  MSH6:  RETAINED (positive nuclear staining in tumor cells)
  PMS2:  RETAINED (positive nuclear staining in tumor cells)

Interpretation: Intact/retained MMR protein expression in all four proteins tested. This result is consistent with a PROFICIENT mismatch repair (pMMR) tumor. This finding is NOT consistent with Lynch syndrome-associated or sporadic MMR-deficient (dMMR) colorectal adenocarcinoma. Microsatellite stability (MSS) is inferred; reflexive PCR-based microsatellite instability testing not additionally indicated per institutional algorithm when all four MMR proteins are retained.

Note: Clinical correlation is recommended. Germline MMR testing may still be considered in appropriate clinical or family history contexts.

---

DIAGNOSIS:

SIGMOID COLON, MASS -- COLONOSCOPIC BIOPSY:

  INVASIVE ADENOCARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (Grade 2 of 3)

  -- Tubular architecture with focal cribriform pattern
  -- Prominent intraluminal dirty necrosis, consistent with colorectal-type morphology
  -- No signet ring cell features identified
  -- No mucinous differentiation
  -- Lymphovascular and perineural invasion: cannot be assessed on biopsy
  -- Immunoprofile consistent with colorectal primary: CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-
  -- MMR proteins: MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2 ALL RETAINED (pMMR; MSS inferred)

See comment.

---

COMMENT:

The morphologic and immunohistochemical features are diagnostic of invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of colorectal type. In the appropriate clinical context (circumferential sigmoid mass at 18 cm from anal verge by colonoscopy), these findings are most consistent with a primary colonic adenocarcinoma arising in the sigmoid colon.

Retention of all four MMR proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) by IHC indicates a proficient mismatch repair (pMMR) tumor, which is the predominant phenotype in sporadic left-sided colon and sigmoid cancers. MSS/pMMR colorectal cancers account for approximately 85% of all cases and are associated with distinct molecular and prognostic features compared to dMMR/MSI-high tumors.

Molecular/genomic testing (KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, and extended RAS/RAF panel) is clinically indicated and has been ordered separately per institutional colorectal cancer molecular profiling protocol. Results will be issued as a separate report.

Specimen also submitted for comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) per tumor board protocol. Results to follow under separate accession.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with a well-established diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3N2aM1b) moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with liver and lung metastases, KRAS exon 2 codon 12 G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, BRAF wild-type. He is now approximately 18-19 months from his initial diagnosis and is currently receiving second-line therapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cyramza).

Briefly, his oncologic history is as follows: He initially presented with synchronous liver-only disease (four hepatic lesions, bilobar) and was treated with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles in conversion/induction intent, achieving a confirmed partial response (CEA nadir 22.8 ng/mL from a baseline of 148.2). He transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab per an OPTIMOX-type strategy. Approximately 10 months into his overall treatment course, surveillance imaging demonstrated disease progression with emergence of three new bilateral pulmonary nodules and hepatic re-growth of the Segment VI and IVb lesions, accompanied by a CEA rise to 67.3 ng/mL. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at that time, achieving a partial response with hepatic and pulmonary disease regression (CEA declined to 44.1 ng/mL). Most recent imaging (approximately 7 months into second-line therapy, last month) demonstrated borderline stable disease with all lesions showing minimal interval increase not meeting threshold for unequivocal progression, and CEA trending modestly upward to 51.8 ng/mL. CA 19-9 was 162 U/mL.

In order to better characterize the molecular evolution of his disease and evaluate for potential acquired resistance mechanisms, a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule was performed approximately one month ago. Pathology confirmed metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary (CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-, TTF-1-). Repeat comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) has now returned and is reviewed in detail at today's visit.

Key findings on repeat NGS: retained baseline alterations (KRAS G12D AF 47%, TP53 R248W, APC E1554*, SMAD4 R361H, CDKN2A homozygous deletion). New/potentially acquired alterations: PIK3CA E545K (AF 18%), KRAS G12V as a secondary subclonal mutation (AF 11%), MYC amplification (CN 8.2), and RB1 Q494* truncating mutation (AF 14%). MSS phenotype retained. ERBB2 not amplified. No NTRK fusions. TMB 6 muts/Mb.

He reports occasional loose stools (1-2 times per day), controlled with as-needed loperamide. No nausea, vomiting, or significant mucositis. No chest pain, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IV (M1b) SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA, KRAS G12D MUTANT, pMMR/MSS -- CURRENTLY BORDERLINE STABLE DISEASE ON SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI/RAMUCIRUMAB

Clinical status: Mr. Kowalski remains with an ECOG performance status of 1, functional and living at home with his wife, although his disease has shown a directional trend toward losing control on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab over the past several months following an initial partial response. His tumor markers are slowly climbing, his LFTs are creeping upward (likely reflecting modest hepatic progression), and the modest size increases on imaging, while not formally meeting any specific progression threshold, paint a picture of slowly evolving disease. It would be reasonable to image again in approximately 4-6 weeks to further characterize the trajectory.

Molecular evolution and implications: Today's NGS results are reviewed in detail with Mr. Kowalski and his wife, in language they can understand. The key takeaway is that his cancer has undergone molecular evolution while on chemotherapy -- this is expected behavior in metastatic colorectal cancer and reflects selective pressure from his prior FOLFOX and current FOLFIRI exposure. Several newly detected alterations are noted:

   - PIK3CA E545K (PI3K pathway activation): This is an acquired or newly detectable mutation seen in approximately 10-15% of mCRC and can be a mechanism of resistance to VEGF-pathway inhibitors and chemotherapy in some settings. There are no immediately actionable FDA-approved therapies targeting PIK3CA E545K in colorectal cancer at this time, though PI3K inhibitors and combinations remain under investigation. Clinical trial participation may be relevant here.
   - KRAS G12V subclone (AF 11%): The emergence of a secondary KRAS-mutant subclone alongside the dominant KRAS G12D suggests intratumoral heterogeneity and clonal evolution. This further confirms that anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) remains contraindicated -- this patient has both a dominant and a subclonal RAS-mutant population.
   - MYC amplification (CN 8.2): High-level MYC amplification is associated with aggressive disease biology and may contribute to chemotherapy resistance. No directly targetable therapy available.
   - RB1 truncating mutation (AF 14%): Emerging RB1 loss has implications for cell cycle regulation and may confer cross-resistance to certain cell cycle-directed agents, though not directly actionable in this context.
   - MSS phenotype retained: Confirmed MSS on repeat biopsy. This means checkpoint inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) remains ineffective and is not an option. There are emerging data on combining checkpoint inhibition with other agents in MSS mCRC (e.g., regorafenib combinations, MEK inhibitors), but none are yet standard of care.
   - ERBB2 not amplified, NTRK fusions absent: No actionable target in these pathways.

Patient was counseled thoroughly that no clearly actionable targetable alteration has emerged, and that his molecular profile does not open new targeted therapy options at this time outside of standard third-line chemotherapy or a clinical trial. He expressed understanding and continued interest in pursuing a clinical trial at the time of next progression. I have agreed to prospectively query available institutional and national trials for molecular eligibility (particularly PI3K pathway trials, KRAS G12D-directed therapy trials -- sotorasib and adagrasib were not developed for colorectal as primary approval but there are combination trials underway). Will refer to our clinical trials office for a dedicated consultation.

Plan for current regimen: Given borderline stable disease -- not clearly progressive, not clearly responding -- we will continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab and repeat CT imaging in 5-6 weeks to clarify the trajectory. If repeat imaging confirms unequivocal progression, we will transition to third-line therapy.

Goals of care discussion reaffirmed today in the presence of Mr. Kowalski and his wife. Patient has a clear and realistic understanding that his disease is not curable with current therapies. His primary goals remain to prolong his life while maintaining quality of life and the ability to remain at home, engage with family, and continue light activities. ACTIONABILITY ASSESSMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TIER I — FDA-APPROVED THERAPY IN THIS TUMOR TYPE / INDICATION
• None identified.
  — KRAS mutations (G12D, G12V): Exclude benefit from cetuximab/panitumumab (negative predictive biomarkers). No FDA-approved KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor is indicated for colorectal cancer at this time.
  — MSS phenotype: Patient does not meet criteria for pembrolizumab (MSI-H/dMMR) or other immune checkpoint inhibitors in first/second-line mCRC.
  — TMB-Low (6 mut/Mb): Does not meet TMB-High threshold (≥10 mut/Mb) for pembrolizumab tumor-agnostic approval.

TIER II — FDA-APPROVED THERAPY IN DIFFERENT TUMOR TYPE / INVESTIGATIONAL IN THIS INDICATION
• PIK3CA p.E545K: PI3K alpha-selective inhibitors (alpelisib) are FDA-approved in PIK3CA-mutant HR+/HER2- breast cancer; not approved in colorectal cancer. No standard role in current mCRC management. May be of interest in basket trial context.
• MYC amplification: No approved MYC-targeted therapies; under investigation (BRD4 inhibitors, CDK inhibitors as indirect MYC pathway strategies).
• RB1 loss + CDKN2A deletion: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are FDA-approved in other indications; loss of RB1 may paradoxically reduce CDK4/6 inhibitor efficacy (RB1 is the effector target). No actionable implication in current setting.

TIER III — CLINICAL TRIAL ELIGIBILITY CONSIDERATIONS
• KRAS G12D mutation: Patient may be eligible for trials investigating MRTX1133 (KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor) or combination KRAS G12D inhibition strategies. Recommend enrollment on available KRAS G12D-directed clinical trial.
• KRAS G12D + PIK3CA E545K co-mutation: Concurrent RAS and PI3K pathway activation may be relevant for trials combining KRAS and PI3K inhibition.
• SHP2 inhibitors (RMC-4630, TNO155) as KRAS pathway co-targeting: Potentially relevant in RAS-mutant tumors regardless of specific allele.
• SMAD4 loss: Associated with inferior outcomes; SMAD4-loss-directed approaches under investigation in preclinical/early clinical settings.

TIER IV — BIOMARKERS OF POTENTIAL BIOLOGIC SIGNIFICANCE (NO CURRENT CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY)
• Subclonal KRAS G12V: Intratumoral heterogeneity; may emerge as dominant clone under continued selective pressure.
• RB1 loss: Cell cycle checkpoint abrogation; potential for synthetic lethality approaches under investigation.
• FBXW7 loss: MYC/NOTCH stabilization; may contribute to resistance phenotype.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERPRETATION SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This post-treatment repeat biopsy specimen from a right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis demonstrates significant molecular evolution compared to the baseline primary tumor profile. The dominant clonal architecture (KRAS G12D, TP53 R248W, APC E1554*, SMAD4 R361H, CDKN2A deletion) is fully preserved, consistent with derivation from the original tumor clone.

Novel acquired alterations include: (1) a subclonal second KRAS G12V mutation suggesting parallel RAS evolution or intratumoral heterogeneity; (2) PIK3CA E545K acquisition, activating a parallel PI3K/AKT/mTOR survival pathway that may contribute to chemotherapy resistance; (3) high-level MYC amplification, representing significant genomic amplification from the low-level 8q gain seen at baseline; and (4) subclonal RB1 truncating mutation, providing an additional cell cycle checkpoint bypass.

The MSS phenotype and low TMB are unchanged, confirming that hypermutation or mismatch repair deficiency has not emerged as an acquired resistance mechanism — a relevant consideration given the FOLFIRI exposure. The ramucirumab target (KDR/VEGFR2) shows no acquired amplification or activating mutation.

Overall, this genomic profile is consistent with clonal evolution under treatment selection pressure, with activation of parallel oncogenic signaling (PI3K pathway) and enhanced proliferative drive (MYC amplification) as potential resistance mechanisms. No immediately actionable FDA-approved therapy is identified in the colorectal cancer setting. Clinical trial enrollment targeting KRAS G12D or RAS pathway co-inhibition strategies is strongly encouraged.

METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Platform: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Cambridge, MA). FDA-approved comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) assay.

Genomic Targets: Hybrid-capture-based next-generation sequencing (NGS) of the entire coding regions of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select intronic regions for rearrangement detection, and whole-genome copy number analysis from the same library. Includes analysis of short variants (substitutions, insertions/deletions), copy number alterations (amplifications and homozygous deletions), structural rearrangements/gene fusions, and whole-genome-derived genomic signatures.

Analysis Includes:
- Short variants: substitutions, insertions/deletions in 324 genes
- Copy number alterations: amplifications and homozygous deletions
- Rearrangements/fusions: select introns of 36 genes
- Tumor mutational burden (TMB): estimated from somatic synonymous and non-synonymous mutations across coding regions
- Microsatellite instability (MSI): assessment of 95 intronic homopolymer repeat loci
- Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): genome-wide assessment

Sequencing Depth: Mean coverage >500x across all targeted regions.
Sensitivity: SNVs/indels ≥5% allele frequency in specimens with ≥20% tumor content; copy number amplifications and homozygous deletions from whole-genome analysis; fusions from targeted RNA-seq for select genes.

DETAILED GENOMIC ALTERATIONS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

** SECTION A: CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT / ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS **

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A)
   Gene/Pathway: KRAS / RAS-MAPK signaling pathway
   Altele Frequency: 42%
   Copy Number Context: Diploid (CN 2.0)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Known oncogenic driver mutation; included in OncoKB Level 1 evidence (FDA-recognized biomarker for anti-EGFR therapy exclusion in colorectal cancer)
   Functional Impact: Activating missense substitution at codon 12 (glycine to aspartate). Constitutively activates downstream RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK signaling, bypassing upstream EGFR receptor control. One of the most common oncogenic drivers in colorectal cancer, occurring in approximately 35-40% of cases.
   Actionability:
     - EXCLUDES benefit from anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody therapies (cetuximab [Erbitux], panitumumab [Vectibix]) per NCCN Category 1 evidence and FDA labeling. RAS mutations (KRAS/NRAS exon 2/3/4) are mandatory biomarker exclusions for anti-EGFR therapy in mCRC.
     - KRAS G12D is also a potential target for novel KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133, investigational) currently in early-phase clinical trials; no FDA-approved KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor available at this time.
     - SOS1 inhibitors and SHP2 inhibitors targeting upstream RAS signaling are under investigation but remain experimental for this mutation in CRC.
   Allele frequency comment: AF 42% is consistent with a heterozygous mutation in tumor cells comprising approximately 60% of the specimen, suggesting dominant clonal presence.

2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Gene/Pathway: TP53 / Cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, DNA damage response
   Allele Frequency: 48%
   Copy Number Context: Likely loss of heterozygosity (AF consistent with LOH at TP53 locus)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Loss-of-function mutation; one of the most common TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer
   Functional Impact: Arginine 248 is a critical DNA contact residue in the p53 DNA-binding domain. The R248W substitution abrogates sequence-specific DNA binding and abolishes transcriptional activation of p53 target genes (p21, BAX, PUMA, MDM2). This variant also exerts dominant-negative activity, inhibiting residual wild-type p53 from co-expressed heteromers. Loss of TP53 function impairs G1/S cell cycle checkpoint, promotes genomic instability, and enables survival under oncogenic stress.
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies directly targeting TP53 loss-of-function in colorectal cancer. APR-246 (eprenetapopt), a TP53 reactivator, has been investigated primarily in hematologic malignancies and limited solid tumor settings; not standard of care. High allele frequency (48%) suggests clonal dominance and/or LOH, consistent with its role as an early event in colorectal carcinogenesis (APC --> KRAS --> SMAD4/TP53 progression model).

3. APC p.E1554* (c.4660G>T)
   Gene/Pathway: APC / WNT-beta-catenin signaling
   Allele Frequency: 51%
   Copy Number Context: Consistent with loss of heterozygosity
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Truncating nonsense mutation; loss of tumor suppressor function
   Functional Impact: APC (adenomatous polyposis coli) encodes a central negative regulator of the WNT/beta-catenin pathway. The nonsense mutation at E1554 produces a truncated protein lacking the armadillo repeat domains, SAMP motifs, and the basic domain, effectively abolishing beta-catenin degradation complex function. This leads to constitutive nuclear accumulation of beta-catenin and transcriptional activation of WNT target genes (MYC, CCND1, MMP7). APC truncating mutations are the initiating driver event in the vast majority of sporadic colorectal cancers (present in ~85% of CRC). The high AF (51%) with apparent LOH is consistent with biallelic inactivation (somatic two-hit model).
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies for APC loss. Investigational approaches include beta-catenin / TCF inhibitors (still early-phase). Not actionable with currently approved agents.

** SECTION B: ADDITIONAL GENOMIC ALTERATIONS OF POTENTIAL SIGNIFICANCE **

4. SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A)
   Gene/Pathway: SMAD4 / TGF-beta signaling
   Allele Frequency: 38%
   Copy Number Context: Chromosome 18q loss (see copy number section below)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- Missense mutation at conserved residue in MH2 domain; loss-of-function predicted
   Functional Impact: SMAD4 (DPC4) is a central mediator of TGF-beta tumor-suppressive signaling. The R361H substitution disrupts the MH2 domain interface required for heteromeric SMAD complex formation and nuclear transcriptional co-activation. SMAD4 loss in CRC is associated with accelerated metastatic progression, resistance to TGF-beta-mediated growth inhibition, and worse clinical outcomes in metastatic disease. SMAD4 loss has been reported in approximately 35% of mCRC cases and is enriched in liver-metastatic disease. Biallelic inactivation is supported by coincident 18q chromosomal loss.
   Prognostic Note: SMAD4 mutation/loss is associated with worse prognosis in Stage IV CRC and has been correlated with shorter progression-free survival on first-line chemotherapy in some series. It may predict for early distant metastasis (consistent with this patient's synchronous liver metastases at diagnosis).
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies for SMAD4 loss in CRC. SMAD4 loss does not predict response to anti-EGFR therapy beyond the dominant RAS exclusion. Under investigation as a marker for response to combined TGF-beta pathway inhibition with immune checkpoint inhibitors (not relevant given MSS status here).

5. CDKN2A -- Homozygous Deletion
   Gene/Pathway: CDKN2A (p16-INK4a / p14-ARF) / Cell cycle regulation (CDK4/6-Rb axis)
   Copy Number: Homozygous deletion (CN ~0), chromosome 9p21 locus
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Complete loss of tumor suppressor function
   Functional Impact: CDKN2A encodes two distinct tumor suppressor proteins via alternative reading frames: p16-INK4a (inhibits CDK4/6, preventing Rb phosphorylation and G1/S entry) and p14-ARF (stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2-mediated ubiquitination). Homozygous deletion abrogates both cell cycle and p53-regulatory tumor suppressive functions. In the context of pre-existing TP53 R248W mutation, the p14-ARF loss may be redundant for p53 pathway disruption, but p16 loss enables unchecked CDK4/6-mediated Rb inactivation. CDKN2A deletion occurs in approximately 20-25% of colorectal cancers.
   Actionability: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are not approved in CRC and clinical trial data in CRC have been disappointing, likely due to context-dependent Rb pathway complexity. No standard actionable implication in this disease type.

6. FBXW7 p.R505C (c.1513C>T)
   Gene/Pathway: FBXW7 / Ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis, MYC/NOTCH/CCNE1 regulation
   Allele Frequency: 22%
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- Recurrently mutated residue (hotspot R465, R479, R505 in WD40 domain)
   Functional Impact: FBXW7 encodes the substrate recognition subunit of the SCF-FBXW7 E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, responsible for targeted degradation of key oncoproteins including MYC, NOTCH1/2, CCNE1, and JUN. The R505C hotspot mutation disrupts substrate recognition, impairing ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of these oncoproteins. Loss of FBXW7 function stabilizes MYC protein and activates NOTCH signaling. The relatively lower allele frequency (22%) may suggest subclonal presence or admixture with normal tissue.
   Actionability: No directly approved therapies targeting FBXW7 loss. MYC protein stabilization resulting from FBXW7 loss may contribute to the concurrent MYC locus gain observed in this tumor (see copy number alterations). Gamma-secretase inhibitors targeting NOTCH have not proven effective in unselected CRC. No standard actionable implication.

INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This comprehensive genomic profiling result is consistent with the known molecular landscape of sporadic left-sided colorectal adenocarcinoma. The dominant molecular profile is characterized by the classical adenoma-to-carcinoma progression pathway: APC truncating mutation (initiating event) --> KRAS activating mutation (early progression) --> SMAD4/TP53 loss (late progression events), consistent with the canonical Fearon-Vogelstein model of colorectal carcinogenesis. The tumor harbors high-frequency clonal mutations in all four of these genes, suggesting a well-established dominant clone.

The KRAS G12D mutation is the most clinically impactful finding: it is a dominant oncogenic driver that definitively excludes this patient from treatment with anti-EGFR antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab), which would otherwise be considered for RAS/BRAF wild-type left-sided mCRC. There are no currently FDA-approved KRAS G12D-targeted agents for colorectal cancer.

The MSS / pMMR status, confirmed by both this genomic assay and diagnostic IHC, indicates the tumor is not expected to respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) or CTLA-4. The low TMB (5 mut/Mb) further supports this conclusion.

SMAD4 R361H loss, combined with 18q chromosomal deletion, represents a biallelic inactivation event with potential prognostic significance in stage IV disease. Published data suggest SMAD4-mutant mCRC may have a more aggressive metastatic phenotype (consistent with synchronous bilobar liver involvement in this patient), though this does not alter first-line therapeutic strategy.

The CDKN2A homozygous deletion and FBXW7 R505C mutation are recurrent alterations in CRC with no currently approved actionable implications.

No targetable alterations have been identified that would guide use of molecularly matched therapies beyond standard chemotherapy backbones. Specifically: no HER2 amplification (excludes trastuzumab/pertuzumab or tucatinib), no NTRK fusion (excludes larotrectinib/entrectinib), no BRAF V600E (excludes BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations), no RET fusion, no ALK/ROS1 fusion, no BRCA1/2 mutation (no PARP inhibitor indication).

SIGNATURE FINDINGS INTERPRETATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mutational landscape of this tumor is consistent with the CRC chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway, characterized by: aneuploidy (chromosomal gains and losses), dominant APC/KRAS/TP53/SMAD4 co-mutations, low TMB, MSS phenotype, and absence of POLE/POLD1 exonuclease mutations. This is the most common molecular subtype of colorectal cancer (approximately 65-70% of cases) and is distinct from the hypermutated MSI-H/TMB-High subtype (approximately 15% of cases) and the POLE-ultramutated subtype (<5%). The CIN pathway tumors have historically lower response rates to immune checkpoint inhibition and derive their primary therapeutic benefit from chemotherapy-based regimens.

The gLOH of 14% is intermediate and, in the context of wild-type BRCA1/2 and absence of HRD-associated mutational signature, does not suggest a homologous recombination deficiency phenotype amenable to PARP inhibition.

Assessment: Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed stage IV (cT3 N2a M1a, AJCC 8th ed.) moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. Currently on maintenance 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab following completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Presenting for routine disease surveillance. Prior imaging available for comparison (most recent CT approximately 4 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (120 mL). Axial images obtained with coronal and sagittal reconstructions. Oral contrast administered. Images reviewed on PACS workstation.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: New finding since prior examination -- three small pulmonary nodules are identified, not present on the comparison study:
- Right lower lobe: A nodule measuring approximately 0.8 x 0.7 cm is seen in the posterior basilar segment. The lesion demonstrates soft tissue density without calcification or satellite nodules.
- Left upper lobe: A second nodule measuring approximately 0.6 x 0.5 cm in the left upper lobe, anterior segment, with similar morphology.
- Right middle lobe: A third, smaller nodule measuring approximately 0.5 x 0.4 cm in the right middle lobe, medial segment.
All three nodules are solid, non-calcified, and rounded in morphology. No cavitation, spiculation, or surrounding ground-glass change. In the setting of this patient's known metastatic colorectal carcinoma with active hepatic disease, these nodules are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastases.
No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No consolidation to suggest superimposed infectious or inflammatory process.

Mediastinum and Hila: No significant mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal. No pericardial effusion. Great vessels are unremarkable. Partially imaged thyroid gland appears within normal limits.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures: Partially imaged upper ribs and spine unremarkable. No destructive osseous lesions. Port-a-cath in place with tip projecting at the cavoatrial junction, unchanged in position.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver shows interval changes compared to the prior examination. The dominant lesion in segment VI of the right hepatic lobe -- previously measuring in the range of 1.6 x 1.4 cm at end of induction -- now measures approximately 2.1 x 1.8 cm. This lesion demonstrates peripheral rim hypoenhancement on portal venous phase with a central hypoattenuating core, morphologically consistent with a metastatic deposit, and appears enlarged compared to prior. In segment IVb, a previously identified small lesion appears to have increased in size, now measuring approximately 1.0 x 0.9 cm (previously approximately 0.7 x 0.5 cm). The small segment II lesion remains visible at approximately 0.6 x 0.5 cm, minimally changed from prior. No new hepatic lesions are identified beyond those previously characterized. Overall, the hepatic disease appears to show interval growth in at least two of the four previously described lesions.
No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. Portal vein patent with appropriate hepatopetal flow on Doppler-appropriate phase. No perihepatic ascites.

Gallbladder and Biliary: Gallbladder is unremarkable, without stones or wall thickening. Bile duct not dilated.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilation or pancreatic lesion identified.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: No adrenal mass.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal cortical defect. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi on review of non-contrast scout. Small bilateral renal cortical cysts, unchanged.

Gastrointestinal Tract -- Primary Tumor: The previously characterized area of sigmoid colon wall thickening is again demonstrated. The lesion appears largely stable in size at approximately 2.9 x 2.5 cm (previously 2.8 x 2.4 cm), with similar degree of mural thickening and luminal narrowing. No evidence of frank bowel obstruction. No perforation or abscess. Pericolonic fat stranding is unchanged, without new mesenteric infiltration. Previously enlarged pericolonic lymph nodes remain resolved.

Lymph Nodes: No new retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No iliac chain adenopathy.

Peritoneum and Mesentery: No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No omental caking.

Vasculature: Aorta and major vessels are patent and of normal caliber.

PELVIS:

Bladder: Unremarkable.
Prostatic Gland: Mildly enlarged without focal lesion.
Bony Pelvis: No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No suspicious focal bony abnormalities.

IMMPRESSION:

1. New bilateral pulmonary nodules, three in total (right lower lobe, left upper lobe, right middle lobe), highly suspicious for pulmonary metastases in this clinical setting. These are new compared with the prior examination and were not present on the staging CT or subsequent surveillance imaging. Clinical correlation with tumor markers and oncology review is advised.

2. Interval enlargement of the segment VI hepatic metastasis and the segment IVb lesion compared with the post-induction examination, indicating hepatic disease progression after a period of response. The segment II lesion appears essentially unchanged.

3. The sigmoid primary tumor remains visible and appears grossly stable, with no new obstructive complications or locoregional changes.

4. No new osseous metastases. No peritoneal disease identified.

5. Port-a-cath in appropriate position.

Overall, findings are most consistent with disease progression with new pulmonary involvement and hepatic worsening in the context of maintained maintenance systemic therapy.

scenario_blurb: Stage IV colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation and synchronous liver metastases in a 58-year-old male. Initial treatment with FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles with partial response, then maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab. Progression at 10 months with new lung nodules, switched to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Develops oxaliplatin neuropathy and treatment-related hypertension.
